exemplar.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained, focused engagement with the prescribed title and knowledge question (Criterion A)
Coherent and relevant use of well-chosen examples from both natural sciences and the arts (Criterion C)
Logical organization of ideas with clear progression from one section to the next (Criterion D)
Presentation of multiple perspectives and balanced treatment of fragility versus resilience (Criterion E)
weaknesses: Depth of critical evaluation is uneven, and broader implications for knowers are only briefly explored (Criterion B)
Limited use of formal TOK terminology reduces conceptual precision (Criterion A/B)
Some examples lack sufficient detail or integration of evidence (Criterion C)
Occasional abrupt transitions and overlapping paragraphs interrupt the flow (Criterion D)
Conclusion is too brief and does not fully synthesise the implications of the arguments (Criterion E)

annotations.comment: When discussing the extension versus fragility of theories, the student could consider ethical or societal consequences of scientific paradigm shifts to add depth to the evaluation.
Relying on Khan Academy as a primary source weakens academic rigour. The student should cite peer-reviewed or foundational scientific literature to support claims about Daltonâs theory.
Citation style is inconsistent and quotations are not fully integrated into the analysis. The student should embed quotes more seamlessly and critique them within the argument.
The explanation of quasicrystals is brief. The student should elaborate on how these structures specifically invalidated assumptions in traditional crystallography to deepen the analysis.
This paragraph overlaps substantially with the Newton-Einstein analysis. The student should synthesize these resilience examples to avoid repetition and strengthen coherence.

The claim that specialized knowledge isnât required to appreciate art is tangential. The student should clarify how this assertion relates to the fragility of artistic knowledge and the knowledge question.
The discussion lacks TOK terminology such as âWays of Knowingâ or âKnowledge Framework,â which would help anchor the analysis in the prescribed TOK concepts and improve conceptual precision.

The conclusion summarizes examples but fails to critically evaluate broader implications for knowers or explain why fragility matters in real-world knowledge production. Greater reflection on consequences is needed.

The opening definition of ârevered knowledgeâ is broad and generic. The student should refine these terms by explicitly linking them to the knowledge question and clarifying what constitutes fragility in this context to set up a sharper analytical framework.

The thesis statement gestures at differing perspectives but does not specify criteria for evaluating fragility or resilience. The student should articulate clear indicators of fragility to guide the subsequent analysis in arts and natural sciences.

The Angkor Wat example is a strong, concrete illustration of reinterpretation in cultural knowledge. Referencing both Britannica and Falser demonstrates engagement with multiple sources to support the claim about shifting scholarly perspectives.

This discussion of Angkor Wat would benefit from deeper evaluation of how reinterpretation affects the knowerâs understanding and societal value judgments, rather than only describing the shift in scholarly opinion.

The counter-argument about enduring cultural significance is asserted rather than analysed. The student should examine why cultural values persist despite new evidence and explore implications for the fragility of collective knowledge.
The transition into the physics section ends mid-thought. The student must complete this sentence and more clearly signal how the natural sciences discussion will develop the fragility theme.

The comparison of Newtonâs law to Einsteinâs relativity is well-structured and effectively shows how new theories can build on or challenge existing ones, illustrating a clear narrative of scientific development.

Analysis here focuses on chronological replacement rather than explaining how Newtonâs conceptual limitations reveal the fragility of revered scientific knowledge. The student should link these limitations directly to the fragility concept.
The sentence regarding technology and materials ends abruptly. The student needs to finish this idea and explain how technological limitations historically masked fragility in scientific theories.

The quasicrystal example aptly demonstrates how technological advances can expose fragility in established scientific methods, showing the studentâs ability to connect new evidence with methodological change.

The discussion acknowledges that Newtonâs laws retain utility alongside Einsteinâs theory, balancing supersession with resilience. This strengthens the argument by showing knowledge can be both supplanted and enduring.

The distinction between building upon and fragility remains underdeveloped. The student should define criteria for when new evidence constitutes a constructive extension rather than indicating fragility of the original theory.
The quasicrystal section would be more persuasive with precise dates and details of the 1984 discovery to underscore the significance of this challenge to classical crystallography.

Using Daltonâs atomic theory to show resilience in scientific knowledge effectively balances earlier discussions of fragility, illustrating that some foundational ideas persist despite new techniques.
At six lines, the conclusion is quite brief. The student should expand it to include forward-looking insights or recommendations on how knowers might navigate the fragility of revered knowledge.
The student presents multiple perspectives in the Angkor Wat discussion, showing awareness of both traditional and revisionist views, which aligns well with evaluating different points of view.

The essay rarely uses formal TOK vocabulary (e.g., scope, perspectives, biases). Integrating these terms would connect analysis more directly to the Theory of Knowledge framework and strengthen academic rigour.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
